output.question: Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.

sources.title: Claude Shannon | Father of Information Theory, American ...

output.findings:
Claude Elwood Shannon was born on April 30, 1916, and passed away on February 24, 2001.

Shannon is widely recognized as the father of information theory, famously established in his 1948 paper, "A Mathematical Theory of Communication." His foundational contributions include the quantification of information through the concept of "entropy," which measures the uncertainty or information content of a source.

Additionally, he established the fundamental limits on data compression (source coding theorem) and the maximum rate at which information can be reliably transmitted over a noisy communication channel (the Shannon-Hartley theorem/channel capacity). His early work also laid the essential groundwork for modern digital circuit design by demonstrating the application of Boolean algebra to electrical switching circuits.

prompt_sent:
You are the Researcher skill. You go to the web for a specific question
and bring back normalised text the rest of the DAG can work from.

Your tool surface is two MCP tools: `web_search(query, max_results)` and
`fetch_url(url)`. Use them. Do not narrate; do not invent other tools.

Procedure:
  1. Read the QUESTION in the prompt.
  2. Issue ONE `web_search` to get candidate URLs.
  3. Pick the 1–3 most authoritative-looking URLs and fetch them with
     `fetch_url` in sequence. Avoid clearly low-signal results (aggregator
     spam, ad redirects).
  4. Synthesise the relevant content from the fetched pages.

Time budget: keep tool calls to 4 max per invocation. If a `fetch_url`
returns very little usable text, do not retry; move on.

Output schema (JSON, no prose, no markdown fences):

  {
    "question": "<the question this run answered>",
    "sources": [{"url": "<url>", "title": "<title>"}, ...],
    "findings": "<2–6 short paragraphs of normalised text>"
  }

You do NOT produce the final user-facing answer. The downstream
distiller or formatter does that. If the question cannot be answered
from the web within your budget, return `"findings": "(not found)"`
and let the next node decide.

USER_QUERY: Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth
date, death date, and three key contributions to information theory.

INPUTS:
[
  {
    "id": "USER_QUERY",
    "kind": "query",
    "value": "Fetch https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Claude_Shannon and tell me his birth\ndate, death date, and three key contributions to information theory."
  }
]
